query: clinical features of biliary atresia in infants including duration of jaundice, stool color, and laboratory markers

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Obstruction of biliary flow : Conditions with this underlying pathophysiology include biliary atresia, choledochal cysts, neonatal sclerosing cholangitis, and neonatal cholelithiasis. Biliary atresia (BA) is the most common cause of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in infants, with an incidence that varies by geographic location. [38] Taiwan, the region with the highest incidence, has a reported frequency of 1 in 6000 live births. In the United States, the incidence is about 1 in 12,000 live births. [39] The etiology of BA is not well understood, but genetic factors, viral infections, toxins, chronic inflammation, and autoimmune injury to bile ducts seem to play a role in the pathogenesis. The disease involves both intra-hepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts and classically presents around 2 to 4 weeks after birth with pale stools and jaundice. The initial ultrasound examination may reveal an absent gallbladder and the classic "triangular cord" sign, a ductal remnant of the extrahepatic bile duct. [40] Early diagnosis is critical to maximizing the response to the Kasai operation (ie, hepatic portoenterostomy). [41] If surgery is delayed until after 90 days of life, <25% of patients are likely to respond, compared to surgery performed within 60 days when >70% of patients will establish adequate bile flow. [42] Choledochal cysts can also cause biliary flow obstruction. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are dilated when choledochal cysts are present, and ultrasonography can detect cysts with normal or dilated intrahepatic bile ducts as opposed to the sclerosed ducts of biliary atresia. However, cystic biliary atresia may resemble choledochal cysts. [43] Neonatal sclerosing cholangitis (NSC) is a rare form of cholangiopathy that presents in infancy with CHB, hepatosplenomegaly, pale stools, and high serum gamma-glutamyltransferase activity (GGT). [44] Neonatal cholelithiasis is another rare entity that causes significant direct hyperbilirubinemia. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Studies in Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Serum aminotransferases may provide evidence of hepatocellular injury in patients with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Alkaline phosphatase and elevated GGT levels may indicate an obstruction in biliary channels. The prothrombin time (PT), the international normalized ratio (INR), and serum albumin evaluate hepatic synthesis and function. Additional tests like TORCH titers for in-utero infections, urine cultures, viral cultures, serologic titers, newborn screening, tests for inborn errors of metabolism, alpha-1 antitrypsin phenotype, and genetic profiles may be diagnostic.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary atresia is an obstructive cholangiopathy of unknown etiology involving both the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. It presents in the neonatal period with persistent jaundice, clay-colored stools, and hepatomegaly. It is fatal if left untreated with a reported survival of less than 10% at 3 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Prognosis -- Successful Achievement of Postoperative Bile Flow
retrieved_docs.content: patients with satisfactory bile flow with good bile clearance after porto-enterostomy (PE) have a better survival outcome with the native liver. Nio et al. reported that most patients who had survived more than 20 years post-PE had normal to moderately elevated liver function test results post-PE. [38] Similarly, Uchida et al. reported that serum AST measured 1-year post-PE is a strong predictive factor for liver dysfunction. [39] Gupta et al. reported that there was a statistically significant decrease in post-PE GGT compared with preoperative levels in patients who had become jaundice-free. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Biliary atresia is a disease of the intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile ducts with an unknown etiology. It presents in neonates with jaundice, clay-colored stool, and hepatomegaly. This activity illustrates the evaluation and management of biliary atresia and explains the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: In biliary atresia, the direct and indirect bilirubin levels are both elevated with the conjugated portion being more elevated. As alkaline phosphatase levels are elevated in children due to bone remodeling, the liver-specific alkaline phosphatase fraction 5' nucleotidase level should be measured. Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGTP) is present in the canalicular bile membrane and is increased in cases of biliary obstruction. GGTP provides a diagnostic accuracy of 50% to 60% in biliary atresia. [18] [19] Serum transaminase levels are mildly elevated.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Studies in Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Radiology may be necessary to evaluate neonatal cholestasis. Hepatic ultrasonography can identify sludging in the biliary tree, gallstones, inspissated bile, and choledochal cysts. A triangular cord sign on hepatic ultrasound has high sensitivity and almost 100% specificity for biliary atresia. [81] Hepatobiliary scintigraphy is another modality utilized to evaluate neonatal cholestasis. Decreased excretion of the tracer 24 hours after introduction suggests obstruction and further differentiates obstructive and nonobstructive causes of cholestasis. [94] Prior treatment with phenobarbital improves the sensitivity of hepatobiliary scintigraphy. However, a liver biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosing neonatal cholestasis. Histopathologic interpretation by an experienced pathologist will identify the correct diagnosis in 90% to 95% of cases and may prevent unnecessary interventions in patients with intrahepatic cholestasis. [95]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Jaundice -- Etiology -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic : There are several genetic etiologies (eg, Alagille syndrome, alpha-1 anti-trypsin deficiency, galactosemia, fructosemia, Tyrosinemia type 1, cystic fibrosis, progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis [PFIC], Aagenaes syndrome, Dubin-Johnson syndrome, bile acid synthesis disorders [BSAD]) that commonly result in CHB. Alagille syndrome (ALGS) is an AD disorder caused by mutations in the JAG1 or NOTCH2 genes, leading to a lack of interlobular bile ducts. [46] With an incidence of 1 in 30,000 live births, ALGS is the most common cause of familial intrahepatic cholestasis, though CHB in patients with ALGS may resolve with age. [47] [35] Characteristic clinical features include butterfly vertebrae, congenital heart defects (eg, peripheral pulmonic stenosis), kidney involvement, dysmorphic features (eg, broad forehead and a small pointed chin), and posterior embryotoxon of the eye. Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels are elevated, often up to 20 times the standard value. Occasionally, patients with cystic fibrosis (CF) present with cholestasis because of abnormal bile that plugs the bile ducts. [48] In developing nations where newborn screening with immunoreactive trypsinogen is unavailable, neonatal cholestasis may be the first clue to diagnosing CF. Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency is the most common genetic cause of cholestasis and can mimic biliary atresia in early infancy. Accumulation of anti-trypsin polymers in the hepatocyte endoplasmic reticulum of a patient with the PIZZ genotype leads to apoptosis of hepatocytes, ultimately resulting in cholestasis and cirrhosis later in childhood. [49] As with ALGS, cholestasis may improve with age. Galactosemia, fructosemia, and tyrosinemia type 1 are a few of the inborn errors of metabolism known to cause cholestasis in neonates. Newborns with galactosemia can present with cholestatic jaundice, cataracts, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, renal tubular acidosis, and Escherichia coli sepsis after ingesting galactose from milk. [50] Galactose-1-phosphate uridyl transferase (GALT) deficiency leads to the accumulation of toxic galactose metabolites in multiple organs. The presence of urine-reducing substances suggests galactosemia, but GALT activity in the liver or erythrocytes confirms the diagnosis. Neonatal cholestasis is a presenting feature in hereditary tyrosinemia type 1, another AR disorder caused by a deficiency of the enzyme fumarylacetoacetate hydroxylase. Other characteristics of this disorder include renal Fanconi syndrome, hepatomegaly, coagulation abnormality, and the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma in untreated older patients. [51] Progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC) is a heterogeneous group of 3 genetic disorders involving canalicular hepatobiliary transport that presents with cholestasis. [52] Types 1 and 2 usually manifest in the neonatal period, while type 3 presents later in infancy. Affected patients with PFIC frequently develop cirrhosis and end-stage liver disease during childhood. In diagnostic studies, the GGT level is within the normal range in types 1 and 2 and elevated in type 3 patients. PFIC type 1 is caused by a mutation in the ATP8B1 gene, which encodes the FIC1 protein, whereas PFIC type 2 is caused by a mutation in the ABCB11 gene, which encodes for the bile salt excretory protein (BSEP). PFIC type 3 is caused by a mutation in the ABCB4 gene, which encodes for the multi-drug resistant-3 protein (MDR3). [53] Aagenaes syndrome, also known as lymphedema cholestasis syndrome (LCS), is another idiopathic familial intrahepatic cholestasis syndrome characterized by neonatal cholestasis and lymphedema in the lower extremities. Aagenaes syndrome is transmitted as an autosomal recessive (AR) trait usually seen in individuals of Norwegian descent. [54] Dubin-Johnson syndrome (DJS) is a rare AR disorder caused by a mutation in the ABCC2 gene, which codes for a non-biliary ion transporter in the liver. A unique feature of DJS is a black-colored liver and the excretion of coproporphyrin 1 in urine. [55] Bile acid synthesis disorder (BASD) results from a deficiency of an enzyme involved in synthesizing bile acids from cholesterol. BASDs are an uncommon cause of cholestasis, but many are curable with medical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Children present in the neonatal period with persisting jaundice, clay-colored stools, and hepatomegaly. [17] Any child with jaundice of over 14 days should no longer be regarded as physiological jaundice and should undergo evaluation. More than half of the children with biliary atresia will have initial pigmented stools that later turn acholic and with the progression of the disease, signs of liver cirrhosis and failure set in with palpable hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, ascites, signs of portal hypertension, and failure to thrive.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: There is no single test that can accurately identify biliary atresia from other causes of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with high specificity. Children undergo both serological and radiological tests along with histopathology to come to a diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Prognosis -- CMV IgM Positive Associated Biliary Atresia
retrieved_docs.content: Those with the poorest outcome and highest risk of death are infants with CMV IgM positive associated biliary atresia.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Prognosis -- Syndromic Variety of Biliary Atresia
retrieved_docs.content: Infants with syndromic BASM respond less well to Kasai surgery and have a poorer overall outcome with a higher risk of death. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Etiology -- Cytomegalovirus (CMV) IgM Positive Biliary Atresia
retrieved_docs.content: This type represents about 10% of the cases, and most of them are non-Whites. These children present with higher bilirubin and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels and more inflammatory infiltrates in the extrahepatic biliary apparatus on histology. This group has the worst prognosis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Evaluation -- Ultrasound (US)
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography is an easily available, non-invasive test and can provide valuable information regarding the liver texture, vasculature patency, and ascites, and can also exclude other causes of obstructive jaundice like a choledochal cyst. [20] [21] Ultrasonography shows a hypoplastic or absent gallbladder. It can be done both in the pre and postprandial state, which will show the non-filling of the gallbladder. It can identify the triangular cord sign that was first described by Park et al. as the presence of a solid proximal biliary remnant in front of the bifurcation of the portal vein. [22] However, some authors disagree on the accuracy and specificity of this sign. Antenatally, infants with a congenital variety of biliary atresia can be detected with an abnormal maternal US scan at around 20 weeks gestation, which can facilitate an early postnatal US and timely referral. Despite the ease and availability of ultrasound, the diagnostic accuracy of this modality for diagnosing biliary atresia is only 78%.

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Alagille syndrome Alpha 1 antitrypsin deficiency TORCH (toxoplasmosis, others, rubella, cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex virus) Caroli disease Choledochal cyst Idiopathic neonatal hepatitis Lipid metabolism disorders Total parenteral nutrition-associated hepatitis

retrieved_docs.title: Biliary Atresia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other studies favor an acquired etiology with seasonal clustering of the cases especially in the winter months and also because 50% of the children with the disease have pigmented stools earlier in life which then later became clay-colored.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: and perinatal jaundice are evaluated by total hemoglobin concentration, and laboratory values below reference ranges support their diagnosis. Neonatal jaundice manifests in multiple specific forms: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), nuclear jaundice (يرقان نووي), nuclear jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي) and physiologic neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). These jaundice types arise from hemolysis, delayed bilirubin conjugation, nutritional factors (breast‑milk) or birth‑timing complications, and they frequently co‑occur with hemorrhagic events. Hemorrhagic disorders anchored in the Neonatal period include loss of umbilical cord clamp leading to bleeding

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is classified as a neonatal condition. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is used to denote a range of disorders that occur in the newborn period, including several distinct types of neonatal jaundice: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory distress), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑associated jaundice). These interrelated neonatal entities share the common context of early‑life physiology and pathology, underscoring the importance of recognizing their overlapping presentations for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: coding, and reporting of important newborn disorders. Collectively, this hierarchical framework integrates liveborn status, gestational duration, fetal growth restriction, reproductive health‑service utilization, perinatal infections, metabolic syndromes, jaundice subtypes, and hemorrhagic disorders within the precise temporal window of the neonatal period, supporting clinicians in categorizing, diagnosing, and managing the interconnected spectrum of perinatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” designates conditions that manifest during the newborn period. It is applied to the occurrence of نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract bleeding), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic neonatal jaundice), and يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns). Recognizing these entities as neonatal helps clinicians focus assessment and management on disorders specific to the early post‑natal timeframe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal (qualifier value) serves as a broad category that includes several distinct neonatal conditions. Within this category, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation is recognized as a specific form of jaundice that manifests in the newborn period. Additional conditions that occur in the neonatal timeframe include نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic or nuclear jaundice), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related neonatal jaundice). Together, these entities illustrate the variety of etiologies—ranging from delayed bilirubin conjugation and breast‑milk factors to prematurity and respiratory bleeding—that can affect newborns, underscoring the importance of recognizing each specific presentation for accurate diagnosis and appropriate neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2268
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal qualifier value encompasses several newborn conditions. It includes umbilical bleeding after birth, which can occur when a loose umbilical cord clamp leads to bleeding. It also occurs in various forms of neonatal jaundice, such as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic jaundice that results from immature liver function in fetuses or newborns, and jaundice that is linked to premature delivery.

Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are conditions that typically occur in the fetal and/or neonatal period and are closely related to the Arabic‑language concept “يرقان وليدي” (neonatal jaundice). Evaluation of these jaundice types relies on the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, which is used to interpret the clinical significance of the findings. In this context, a laboratory result that falls below the reference range is interpreted as an important indicator when assessing hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2323
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified) is a neonatal condition that, together with a range of other neonatal disorders, defines the health challenges encountered during the neonatal period. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, postpartum hemorrhage (نزف سري بعد الولادة), neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), and jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان) all occur within the neonatal timeframe. Recognizing that these entities—bacterial sepsis of the newborn, delayed‑conjugation jaundice, postpartum hemorrhage, respiratory tract hemorrhage, breast‑milk‑induced jaundice, and prematurity‑related jaundice—are interrelated manifestations of neonatal physiology and pathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention, and optimal neonatal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان); and * Jaundice specifically caused by Rh alloimmunization in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice subtypes are often evaluated together with total hemoglobin concentration, where values below the reference range support the diagnosis of hemolytic disease. Recognizing the interrelated nature of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, hemolytic processes, diverse jaundice mechanisms and hemorrhagic events—along with appropriate laboratory interpretation—is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention and optimal care of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, also referred to by the Arabic term “يرقان وليدي,” can occur during the fetal and/or neonatal period and may be observed throughout the broader period of life between birth and death, including the specific stage of infancy. In clinical assessment, a total hemoglobin concentration that falls below the reference range is interpreted as indicative of hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice, and the measurement of total hemoglobin concentration is used to interpret the presence of this condition. Recognizing the timing of occurrence, the laboratory interpretation of low hemoglobin, and the synonymous terminology enhances patient evaluation and care.
